content: {"perspective": "Scientific", "answer": "The search results confirm that meta-regression and subgroup analyses have identified several consistent moderators of the high heterogeneity in IF RCT outcomes, but no single snippet provides a complete I2-resolution table across the MetS endpoint panel. The Wiley Obesity Reviews paper by Shen et al. (Source 3, onlinelibrary doi:10.1111/obr.13855) on longer-term IF effects on body composition and cardiometabolic outcomes states explicitly that 'subgroup analyses showed that the mode of IF and intervention duration were the primary moderators of IF effects on the markers' — directly pointing to protocol type (TRE vs ADF vs 5:2 vs IER) and intervention length as the top-level heterogeneity sources. The Nutrition Reviews 16/8 TRE meta-analysis (Source 5, doi:10.1093/nutrit/nuaf206) adds participant sex, physical activity level, and intervention duration as moderators for the 16/8 TRE effect on glucose, HOMA-IR, insulin, and HDL-C. A Nature Int J Obes meta-analysis (Source 7, doi:10.1038/s41366-024-01704-2) on TRE plus exercise used BMI, exercise type, intervention duration, and energy intake as moderator variables for body composition change. A medRxiv subgroup analysis on early vs midday vs late TRE (Source 8) reports that shorter intervention durations are associated with BETTER outcomes — a counter-intuitive finding likely driven by dropout-attrition confounding. The MDPI Nutrients review (Source 14) concludes TRE fat loss effects depend on eating window length and intervention strategy. The ScienceDirect paper by Brokaw et al. (Source 1, doi 10.1016/j.obmed.2025) provides a dedicated meta-analysis and meta-regression specifically for IF effects. Information gap: the snippets do NOT disclose the specific I2 reduction after moderator adjustment for any endpoint, nor do they provide a MetS-specific moderator table. The search-level evidence converges on PROTOCOL TYPE, INTERVENTION DURATION, EATING WINDOW LENGTH, SEX, and BASELINE METABOLIC STATUS as the dominant moderators, but quantitative heterogeneity-resolution data require full-text reading of Shen 2024 (Obesity Reviews), Nutrition Reviews 16/8 TRE 2024, and the Brokaw et al. meta-regression paper.", "key_findings": ["Shen et al. 2024 Obesity Reviews (Source 3): the mode of IF and intervention duration are identified as the PRIMARY moderators of IF effects on body composition and cardiometabolic markers.", "Nutrition Reviews 16/8 TRE meta-analysis (Source 5): participant sex, physical activity level, and intervention duration are reported as moderators for the 16/8 TRE effect on fasting glucose, HOMA-IR, insulin, and HDL-C.", "Nature IJO 2024 TRE+exercise meta-analysis (Source 7): BMI, exercise type, intervention duration, and energy intake were the moderator variables tested for body composition.", "MedRxiv early/midday/late TRE subgroup analysis (Source 8): shorter intervention durations are associated with BETTER outcomes across groups — likely driven by dropout-attrition confounding rather than true biological decay.", "No snippet reports the specific I2 reduction after moderator adjustment for any endpoint in the MetS-specific literature. Quantitative heterogeneity resolution requires full-text reading of the identified meta-regression papers."], "sources_used": ["https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S1871402125000967", "https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12076832/", "https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/obr.13855", "https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/nutrition/articles/10.3389/fnut.2026.1772836/full", "https://academic.oup.com/nutritionreviews/advance-article/doi/10.1093/nutrit/nuaf206/8373435", "https://www.nature.com/articles/s41366-024-01704-2", "https://www.medrxiv.org/content/10.64898/2026.01.29.26345140v1.full-text", "https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10594936/", "https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12829361/", "https://www.mdpi.com/2072-6643/16/19/3390"]}
